hook: A one-hundred-dollar cheesesteak put a Philadelphia restaurant on the map without a dollar of advertising. Jonah Berger spent years proving that what spreads an idea is not luck or a celebrity, but six ingredients you can build into almost anything.

p: In 2005 a Philadelphia restaurant called Barclay Prime put a one-hundred-dollar cheesesteak on the menu, made with thinly sliced Kobe beef, shaved truffles, seared foie gras, and a small bottle of chilled champagne. People could not stop talking about it. It got written up on television and in newspapers and put the restaurant on the map, with no advertising at all. The dish was so remarkable that customers did the marketing for free, one conversation at a time.

Berger opens with a number that reframes everything: word of mouth drives twenty to fifty percent of all purchasing decisions. It beats advertising for two reasons. It is more persuasive, because your friends have no reason to lie the way an ad does. And it is targeted, because you naturally tell the friend who hikes about the great new trail, so the message finds the people who already care.

Then Berger picks a fight with conventional wisdom. Marketers chased influentials, the connectors and mavens with huge networks, sure that spread depended on a special few. But the evidence is thin. Most word of mouth comes from ordinary people, and the average person talks about brands more than a dozen times a day. So the real question is not who is talking. It is what makes something worth talking about.
sections.pull: Virality is not born. It is made.

sections.h: Social Currency And The Secret Bar
p: The first principle is Social Currency: we share things that make us look good. The things we mention are a signal, just like the clothes we wear or the car we drive. If talking about your product makes someone look sharp, clever, or in the know, they will talk about it, because sharing it quietly polishes their own reputation.

Berger's example is a New York bar called Please Don't Tell. You walk into a hot dog shop, step inside an old wooden phone booth, pick up the antique receiver and dial three, and a hidden door swings open into a dark cocktail bar. The place does no advertising, yet people cannot wait to tell others, because knowing the secret makes them feel like an insider. The very secrecy is what makes it spread.

Remarkable things get shared because they break the pattern of the ordinary. Blendtec, a maker of unglamorous blenders, ran a video series called "Will It Blend?" in which the founder pulverized marbles, a rake handle, and a brand new iPhone, then held up the jar of black dust and deadpanned not to breathe it. Hundreds of millions of views later, sales climbed. The blender was ordinary. The demonstration was remarkable, and remarkable is shareable.

The second principle is Triggers: top of mind, tip of tongue. A trigger is a cue in the environment that reminds people of your idea, because we talk about whatever is activated in our heads, and what is activated is driven by what surrounds us. In 1997 sales of the Mars bar mysteriously rose while the company had done nothing. NASA's Pathfinder had just landed on the planet Mars, filling the news, and the planet kept nudging the candy bar into people's minds.

Berger contrasts two songs. Rebecca Black's "Friday" was mocked as one of the worst ever recorded, yet every single week its plays spike on Fridays, because the day itself is a trigger that reactivates the song. A brilliant song with no recurring cue gets a bigger first burst and then fades. A link to a frequent trigger can be worth more than pure quality.

Kit Kat had gone flat until a campaign deliberately welded it to coffee, a break with a Kit Kat and a cup of coffee, repeated until the two were linked in people's minds. Coffee is a superb trigger because millions of people drink it every day, often several times. Sales climbed, not because the candy got tastier, but because it gained a daily cue. So do not only ask whether your message is clever. Ask what in your customer's daily life will bring it back to mind.

The third principle is Emotion: when we care, we share. But Berger uncovers a twist most people miss, because not all emotions spread. With Katherine Milkman he analyzed thousands of New York Times articles to see which ones reached the most-emailed list. The dividing line was not positive versus negative. It was arousal, how much an emotion fires up the body.

High-arousal emotions drive sharing: awe, excitement, amusement, and on the negative side anger and anxiety. All of them rev you up, and that energy spills over into action. Sadness does the opposite, because it is low arousal and deactivating, so it actually makes people less likely to share. The goal is not to make people feel good, or simply to make them feel. It is to make them feel activated.

Susan Boyle walking onstage to smirks and then singing gloriously spread on pure awe. Dave Carroll, ignored for months after United Airlines baggage crews broke his guitar, wrote a pointed song called "United Breaks Guitars," and tens of millions shared it on high-arousal anger at faceless corporations. Berger even found that people made to jog in place shared an unrelated article more often, because the body's arousal alone spilled over into the urge to share.

The fourth principle is Public: built to show, built to grow. Human beings imitate what they can see. Facing uncertainty, we assume that if many people are doing something, it is probably the smart choice. But you can only copy what is visible. If a behavior stays private, the herd instinct has nothing to feed on, so making your idea observable is one of the most powerful moves you can make.

Steve Jobs flipped the Apple logo on the laptop lid so it faced away from the user. Open your Mac in a cafe and the glowing apple faces the whole room, turning every user into a small billboard. Apple made its earbuds white when every other pair was black, so the trailing white cords showed the street who owned an iPod. Movember does the same with mustaches, which you cannot hide, so for a month each one invites the question, why the mustache, and every question spreads the cause.

But going public can backfire. A famous anti-drug campaign hammered home how many teenagers face drugs, and researchers found it may have made things worse. By showing how common drug use was, it broadcast the social proof that everyone is doing it, the opposite of its intent. Make sure the behavior you make visible is the one you actually want to spread.
sections.pull: Built to show is built to grow.

p: The fifth principle is Practical Value: news you can use. We share useful things to help the people we care about, a small act of caring that says, I was thinking of you. Berger's favorite example is an eighty-six-year-old man named Ken Craig demonstrating how to shuck corn cleanly, microwave the ear, cut the end, and shake the cob out silk and all. A homely kitchen video, shared millions of times, purely because it was genuinely useful.

Saving money is practical value too, but our sense of a deal is not rational. It depends on a reference point. Williams-Sonoma sold almost none of its home bread machine until it added a bigger, pricier model right beside it. The expensive one barely sold, but sales of the original nearly doubled, because now it looked like the sensible mid-priced choice. The comparison made it a deal.

Berger offers a tool you can use today, the Rule of 100. Below one hundred dollars, percentage discounts sound bigger, so twenty percent off a twenty-five-dollar shirt beats five dollars off. Above one hundred, dollar amounts win, so two hundred dollars off a two-thousand-dollar laptop beats ten percent off. He adds that a tip aimed at a narrow group often spreads better inside it, because it feels made for exactly that person.

The sixth principle is Stories: information travels under the guise of idle chatter. People do not think in facts and bullet points, they think in stories, and a good tale carries information along like a stowaway. Berger reaches for the Trojan Horse. The story is the horse, your message is the soldiers hidden inside, and people happily wheel it past the defenses they normally raise against being sold to.

Subway built a campaign around Jared Fogle, a college student who lost over two hundred pounds eating lower-fat Subway sandwiches every day. Nobody repeats grams of fat to a friend, but they will retell the story of the man who lost half his body weight, and the health message rides along inside it. It worked because the message was woven into the story, not stuck on the outside.

The warning is Evian and the roller-skating babies, a computer-generated ad of babies breakdancing on skates. Tens of millions watched and shared it, yet Evian's sales barely moved, and many sharers could not even recall the brand. The babies were delightful but irrelevant to the water. The test is simple. If someone can tell your story without naming your product, the message will fall off in transit.
sections.pull: If they can leave you out, you built roller-skating babies.

takeaways: Run whatever you are trying to spread through all six STEPPS as a checklist, and consciously strengthen at least two or three of them.

Find a frequent daily cue, a time of day, a place, an object, or a habit like the morning coffee Kit Kat grabbed onto, and deliberately link your message to it until the cue brings you to mind on its own.
Make your product remarkable enough that talking about it makes the person look like an insider or someone in the know.
Give people something visible to show they use or support you, a badge, a distinctive color, a signature line, or a token, the way white earbuds signaled an iPod.
Aim for a high-arousal emotion such as awe, excitement, amusement, or even righteous anger, and avoid lukewarm or purely sad messaging that deactivates people.
Do the roller-skating-babies test on your story: if someone can retell it without naming your product, rewrite it so the message is load-bearing and the story collapses without it.
Stop hunting for an influential messenger and spend that energy making the message itself worth passing on.
